Pearson said he is building on his success organizing events for young conservatives to launch Next250, a political action committee aimed at countering support for socialism and DEI on college campuses.

His PAC will go head-to-head with the ideology embraced by younger students by rallying conservatives in deep blue areas and swing states for voter registration.

He sees partnering with fraternities and sororities as a way to build a force capable of pushing back against socialism and DEI.

"We're going to take this MAGA party on the road. We're going go to swing states like Georgia, Ohio, Michigan, Iowa. And we're going to throw these events and we're going to welcome young people around to them and we are going to have voter registration. We are going get them to the polls," said Pearson.

He added that the left's embrace of socialism and DEI candidates has already backfired for them.

The left wants to "accuse us being a Nazi. Well, that man had a Nazi tattoo on his chest and Democrats had no problem with it," said Pearson referencing former Maine Senate nominee Graham Platner who withdrew his candidacy after a bombshell rape allegation.

Pearson also plans to deploy $1 million for digital ads that target both young and minority voters in his home state of Georgia.

"America has been on a generational run, a 250-year run that was marketed by innovation, opportunity, and truly the hallmark and the pillars of the American dream. But if the left gets their way, there will not be another 250 years," said Pearson.

item.title: DOJ gives New Jersey 5 days to hand over voter data after noncitizen registration scandal
item.description: Harmeet Dhillon gave Gov. Mikie Sherrill five days to hand over voter data on 6,600 noncitizen registrations as the DOJ escalates its lawsuit.
item.content:encoded:
Assistant Attorney General Harmeet Dhillon doubled down on the DOJ's demand for New Jersey's voter registration data Wednesday after Democratic Gov. Mikie Sherrill's public revelation that around 6,600 noncitizens were added to the rolls through the motor-voter process.

Dhillon’s letter to Sherrill came as a slew of lawmakers called for audits and bipartisan cooperation to address the findings, while the Justice Department noted it remains in litigation against Lt. Gov. Dale Caldwell seeking New Jersey’s electronic voter database.

"Ensuring that U.S. citizens' votes are not illegally diluted by noncitizens' votes is of paramount importance," Dhillon wrote.

"We therefore write to formally request additional information to assess New Jersey's compliance with various federal statutes, as provided in our July 7, 2026, letter to the Lieutenant Governor. As we stated in that letter, we stand ready to help New Jersey election officials comply with the Help America Vote Act of 2002 and the National Voter Registration Act of 1993, as outlined in our memorandum titled, Federal Law Requirements for State and Local Election Officials."

DEMOCRAT NEW JERSEY GOVERNOR SAYS THOUSANDS OF NONCITIZENS DISCOVERED ON VOTER ROLLS, PURGED

Dhillon noted that about 400 of the roughly 6,600 noncitizens New Jersey said were mistakenly registered to vote and cast ballots.

She gave Sherrill five days to provide the names, personal information, registration dates and precincts of the 6,600, as well as the methods and times by which the 400 who voted cast their ballots, while treating the letter as a legal preservation-of-records request typical in litigation.

With the DOJ already engaged in litigation against Caldwell and the state, Dhillon said she would notify the judge in that case of the new development as the department continues to seek statewide voter registration lists from states including New Jersey, New York and Pennsylvania.

NEW JERSEY REMOVES 6,600 PEOPLE FROM VOTER ROLLS WHO WERE ELIGIBLE TO CAST A BALLOT FOR 2024 ELECTION

When reached for comment, the DOJ provided a separate letter Deputy Attorney General Jesus Osete sent to Caldwell and New Jersey Division of Elections Director Donna Barber on Wednesday, again requesting electronic voter registration data.

Osete said the DOJ has grounding to "ascertain New Jersey's compliance with the list maintenance requirements" of the National Voter Registration Act of 1993 and the Help America Vote Act of 2002.

He added that the New Jersey Division of Elections must also verify that no additional noncitizens are registered on the state's voter rolls.

The letter was filed as an exhibit in the Justice Department's ongoing lawsuit against New Jersey seeking access to the state's electronic voter registration database.

Meanwhile, the French technology contractor that works with the State Motor Vehicle Commission (MVC) said late Wednesday that responsibility lies not with the MVC but with the Department of State, led by Caldwell, and the Division of Elections.

Sherrill spokesman Sean Higgins slammed the DOJ in comments to Fox News Digital on Thursday, saying the Trump administration has a "long history of weaponizing" the agency for political purposes and of attempting to "overturn election results."

"Governor Sherrill has been clear that she will use all the legal tools available to her to protect New Jerseyans' personal information," Higgins said, pointing again to Sherrill's public remarks Wednesday and noting the governor's "disgust" at the vendor and former Gov. Philip Murphy's administration.

IDEMIA said in a statement it has worked with the MVC for four decades to process eligible individuals seeking to register to vote when conducting drivers licensing business.

SIGN UP TO GET THE POLITICS NEWSLETTER

"IDEMIA’s role is to transmit information through the motor vehicle system. The voter registration information is transmitted to the New Jersey Department of State, Division of Elections, which is ultimately responsible for verifying eligibility to vote," said the company — whose American base is in Reston, Virginia.

"Information submitted by IDEMIA must still be validated and adjudicated by the Division of Elections."

Meanwhile, the top Republican on the state's Judiciary Committee said she has tried multiple times to press the issue of voter-roll integrity with Democrats — who control all branches of government — since 2019.

"I have been warning... New Jersey needed stronger safeguards to protect the integrity of our elections," state Sen. Kristin Corrado, R-Totowa, said.

"I sent six separate letters raising concerns and calling for action and introduced legislation that would have provided a commonsense safeguard by preventing the MVC from prompting non-citizens to register to vote but my warnings were repeatedly ignored by the Democrats," she said in a statement that included some of the letters sent to the Murphy administration.

"Now we are learning that a catastrophic government failure allowed thousands of non-citizens to be registered to vote, and suddenly everyone is acting surprised," she said.

The DOJ appeared to signal that the New Jersey case strengthens the Trump administration's push for stronger election integrity measures nationwide as multiple states continue to resist requests for information sharing.

item.title: Pat McAfee forced to address ESPN layoffs on air during awkward segment, but the answer is simple
item.description: JJ Watt joked about ESPN layoffs on Pat McAfee's show, asking if everybody was fired to pay his reported $30 million salary and potential raise.
item.content:encoded:
Things look a little different at ESPN today. Haven't you heard?

The World Wide Leader laid off dozens of employees this week, including Ryan Clark, Cam Newton, Tom Pelissero and Karl Ravech. Others were let go as well, but those are some of the big names circulating the internet as of today.

This isn't the first round of layoffs at ESPN — or for any company. Frankly, this is at least the third or fourth round of layoffs at ESPN since I started working at OutKick four years ago. It's become a yearly story at this point.

EXCLUSIVE: ESPN TO PART WAYS WITH CAM NEWTON AS PART OF TALENT LAYOFFS THAT ALSO INCLUDED RYAN CLARK

Anyway, something else that's become a yearly topic is why ESPN is letting folks go, with several people placing the blame on Pat McAfee. That's right. Pat McAfee!

He earns roughly $30 million a year, and is reportedly in negotiations with ESPN to double that moving forward. Obviously, those numbers are going to raise some eyebrows when these layoffs happen.

NFL Hall of Famer JJ Watt joined the "progrum" on Tuesday, and ... didn't shy away from the elephant in the room:

"Slow, slow news day over there on the ESPN airwaves, huh?" Watt said to begin the interview. "I mean, besides the fact that you’re getting $100 million… Did they keep anybody? Or is everybody fired to pay you? I’m trying to figure that out."

Goodness gracious. Just a vicious way to start an interview. Brutal stuff.

Look, it was obviously in jest, and McAfee responded by laughing it off. He's not stupid. He's not immune to the narrative on social media right now.

"We do not appreciate that," he said. "That is the narrative, and I’m certainly taking it on the shins. The boys and I are very proud of the work that we have done. Thanks."

Here's the bottom line: ESPN views Pat McAfee as an asset. The network has put him everywhere since the two partnered up a few years back.

He's on College GameDay. He's on the sidelines for the biggest college football games, sometimes even getting his own simulcast feed. His show is on the main channel every day. He travels to big sporting events outside of football for his show.

Just last week, he was at the Home Run Derby, which was notable given it was the first year it wasn't on ESPN in decades.

It's clear that ESPN believes in McAfee and wants to continue to invest in him. It's probably not a bad investment to make given he's arguably the biggest all-around sports personality in the media.

He's also self-made. ESPN did not create Pat McAfee. ESPN invested in McAfee — and his team — years ago. Since he joined College GameDay in 2022, ratings have gone up. It's not all because of McAfee, of course, but that's not a coincidence.

"The first time we licensed the show through ESPN, one of these happened, we got blamed," McAfee said later in the interview. "This time, we’re getting blamed as well. Which I guess I can understand, but I mean, there’s billions and billions of dollars in these companies, and hard decisions have to get made.

"And we don’t like any of them, and we hope we’re an asset to the company going forward."

And that's what McAfee is to ESPN — an asset. A big one. He's proven that, time and time again, since joining the network. Think about all the big interviews you see out of the World Wide Leader right now ... where do they come from?

The Pat McAfee show. Not First Take. Not NFL Live. Not SportsCenter.

McAfee's radio show.

Think of all the viral moments we see every Saturday during the fall. Where do they come from?

McAfee's kicking segment during College GameDay. Or McAfee's outlandish intro during the opening scene.

Like it or hate it — and I know plenty of folks hate it — it's clearly working.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

SIGN UP TO GET THE LATEST SPORTS NEWS

Pat McAfee didn't cost Ryan Clark his job. Ryan Clark lost his job because he brought nothing to the table for ESPN, beyond an occasional headache because he said something stupid.

ESPN chose to cut ties with a liability, and put more money into an asset. That's not a new or novel concept.

It's how businesses have been run for years.
